provenance.source_corpus: Trappings of Success: Predator Removal for Duck Nest Survival in Alberta Parklands

source_spans.text: Predator removal during the nesting season has increased duck nest survival on township-sized (9324 ha) areas of agricultural ecosystems in eastern locations of the prairie pothole region (PPR).
Predator removal during the nesting season has increased duck nest survival on township-sized (9324 ha) areas of agricultural ecosystems in eastern locations of the prairie pothole region (PPR).
Estimates of 34-day nest survival did not significantly differ between trapped (x = 20.9%, 95% CI = 13.2%–33.7%) and control (x = 17.8%, 95% CI = 10.5%–30.0%) plots in any year.
Controlling for year and transposing treatment, the common odds of nesting hen mortality due to predation were significantly higher in trapped plots
Though predator removal did not depress predator abundance sufficiently to improve nest survival
Estimates of 34-day nest survival did not significantly differ between trapped (x = 20.9%, 95% CI = 13.2%–33.7%) and control (x = 17.8%, 95% CI = 10.5%–30.0%) plots in any year.
Controlling for year and transposing treatment, the common odds of nesting hen mortality due to predation were significantly higher in trapped plots
Though predator removal did not depress predator abundance sufficiently to improve nest survival
indicating that nest survival was driven by habitat characteristics
indicating that nest survival was driven by habitat characteristics
2017 Viking control plot was negatively affected by nest marking
2017 Viking control plot was negatively affected by nest marking

Mesopredator release, the expansion of distribution or density of a mid-trophic level predator resulting from the decline of an apex predator (in our system, coyotes) [ 83,84], is known to benefit foxes

Mesopredator release, the expansion of distribution or density of a mid-trophic level predator resulting from the decline of an apex predator (in our system, coyotes) [ 83,84], is known to benefit foxes
Coyotes limit red fox abundance through interference competition that includes direct killing and instilling fear
Coyotes limit red fox abundance through interference competition that includes direct killing and instilling fear
